title: RKKY interaction in altermagnets with adiabatic electron-phonon coupling
abstract: Altermagnets, characterized by time-reversal symmetry breaking without net magnetization and momentum-dependent spin-split bands, offer a promising platform for spintronics due to their anisotropic spin textures and potential for tunable magnetic interactions. Here, we theoretically investigate the slow phonon-renormalized Ruderman-Kittel-Kasuya-Yosida (RKKY) interaction in two-dimensional Rashba $d$-wave altermagnets, incorporating arbitrary Rashba spin-orbit coupling (RSOC) strength and spin-dependent static-Holstein electron-phonon coupling (EPC). Using a continuum model Hamiltonian that captures altermagnetic anisotropy, RSOC, and adiabatic lattice distortions, we compute the noncollinear RKKY exchange tensor via second-order perturbation theory and numerical momentum-space integration. Our results reveal that static lattice distortions provide a versatile tuning knob for engineering the magnitude, anisotropy, and chirality of RKKY couplings: moderate EPC suppresses long-range coherence while inducing component-specific phase shifts and sign reversals, particularly in Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya and compass-like terms, enabling control over ferromagnetic/antiferromagnetic alignments and clockwise/counterclockwise chiralities. Systematic parameter scans demonstrate enhanced oscillatory complexity with altermagnetic order, RSOC, and doping. These findings establish slow phonons as a low-energy tuning knob that systematically controls the magnitude, anisotropy, phase, and chirality of noncollinear RKKY couplings - even at arbitrary Rashba strength - thereby providing a practical route to engineer ferromagnetic/antiferromagnetic alignments and clockwise/counterclockwise DM chiralities in altermagnet-based heterostructures.
summary: This paper explores how lattice vibrations (phonons) can be used to control magnetic interactions in altermagnets. By considering the coupling between electrons and slow phonons, the authors show that one can tune the strength and direction of the RKKY interaction. This includes the ability to switch between ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic alignments and control the chirality of spin textures. These findings suggest a practical way to engineer complex magnetic states in altermagnet-based spintronic devices.
main_problem: Investigating how adiabatic electron-phonon coupling (EPC) and Rashba spin-orbit coupling (RSOC) modulate the noncollinear RKKY exchange interaction in 2D altermagnets.
main_result: Static lattice distortions act as a tuning knob to control the magnitude, anisotropy, and chirality of RKKY couplings, enabling the engineering of ferromagnetic/antiferromagnetic alignments and DM chiralities.
method: Second-order perturbation theory and numerical momentum-space integration using a Green's function-based approach within a continuum model.
model_or_system: A 2D Rashba d-wave altermagnet model incorporating spin-dependent static-Holstein-type electron-phonon coupling and Rashba spin-orbit coupling.
key_observables: Noncollinear RKKY exchange tensor components, including Heisenberg, Ising-like, Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM), and compass-type interactions.
important_parameters: Rashba spin-orbit coupling strength (alpha), altermagnetic anisotropy (delta), electron-phonon coupling strength (lambda), and Fermi energy (epsilon_F).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the adiabatic (slow phonon) limit, zero temperature, and d-wave symmetry.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows electronic dispersion with and without RSOC; Figure 3 shows spatial dependence of RKKY components with varying EPC; Figure 6 shows RKKY components as a function of EPC and RSOC; Figure 7 shows dependence on Fermi energy.
paper_structure: The paper introduces altermagnetic properties, defines a continuum Hamiltonian with RSOC and EPC, derives the RKKY exchange tensor via perturbation theory, and performs systematic parameter scans (RSOC, EPC, and doping) to demonstrate tunability.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Low-energy electronic dispersion, i.e., the band struc- ture, along the Y ←Γ →X high-symmetry path of the first Brillouin zone for a 2D Rashba dx2−y2-wave altermagnet de- scribed by Eq. (2), shown in the absence (left panel) and in the presence (right panel) of RSOC α = 0.5 eV·Å.

FIG. 2. (a,b) Angular dependence of the RKKY tensor com- ponents Jij for a pristine 2D Rashba dx2−y2-wave altermagnet without EPC as a function of the relative orientation angle ϕR between two magnetic impurities separated by R = 30 Å. (a) Heisenberg and Ising components Jxx, Jyy, and Jzz exhibit a periodic oscillatory behavior with ϕR reflecting the underly- ing spin-split Fermi surface anisotropy. (b) Anisotropic and DM components show finite angular modulations (still peri- odic) arising from the interplay between altermagnetic spin- splitting and spin-orbit coupling. (c,d) Distance dependence of the corresponding RKKY couplings for fixed ϕR = π/3. (c) The diagonal terms display...

FIG. 3. Spatial dependence of RKKY exchange components for several values of EPC λ in a 2D Rashba dx2−y2-wave al- termagnet. Parameters: δ = 0.5, α = 0.2 eV·Å, εF = 0, ˜λ/λ = 1/4, and ϕR = π/3. (a) In-plane symmetric exchange Jxx+yy = Jxx + Jyy, (b) out-of-plane Ising component Jzz, (c) off-diagonal symmetric (compass) term Jxy, and (d) anti- symmetric (DM) combination JDM,x+DM,y = JDM,x + JDM,y. Increasing EPC (λ) reduces the long-range coherence and, be- yond a moderate coupling strength, suppresses all exchange contributions.

Figure 3 shows the spatial dependence of distinct com- ponents of the indirect exchange interaction between two localized magnetic moments, obtained from an RKKY calculation based on the Rashba-altermagnet model in- cluding EPC. Other model parameters are held fixed at δ = 0.5, α = 0.2 eV·Å, εF = 0, ˜λ/λ = 1/4, and direc- tion ϕR = π/3. To better distinguish the in-plane and out-of-plane contributions and to avoid redundant fea- tures in the plots, we next present the combined in-plane components Jxx+yy and JDMx+DMy. Figure 3(a) displays the combined in-plane sym- metric exchange, which largely captures the isotropic (Heisenberg-like) component of the RKKY tensor in the plane. For λ = 0 the...

FIG. 4. Distance dependence of the phonon-renormalized RKKY interaction components in a 2D Rashba dx2−y2-wave altermagnet for different ˜λ, denoting the EPC to the spin-split bands. The parameters are δ = 0.5, α = 0.2 eV·Å, εF = 0, λ = 0.15 eV/Å, and ϕR = π/3. Increasing ˜λ both renormal- izes amplitudes and shifts the oscillation phases, producing component-specific enhancement, suppression, and even sign changes.

FIG. 5. Evolution of the different components of the RKKY exchange interaction as a function of the EPC strength λ for different values of the altermagnetic order parameter δ. In- creasing δ leads to a pronounced enhancement of both the am- plitude and complexity of the oscillations in exchange chan- nels, reflecting the strong modulation of phonon-mediated interactions by the altermagnetic order. Parameters: α = 0.2 eV·Å, εF = 0, ˜λ/λ = 1/4, ϕR = π/3, and R = 30 Å.

FIG. 6. Dependence of the RKKY interaction components on the EPC strength λ for different RSOC strengths α. Here, δ = 0.5, εF = 0, ˜λ/λ = 1/4, ϕR = π/3, and R = 30 Å. For vanishing RSOC (α = 0), the RKKY interactions are pre- dominantly isotropic, with the in-plane Heisenberg and out- of-plane Ising terms showing small amplitude oscillations as a function of λ. Increasing α enhances anisotropic effects, leading to larger amplitudes and pronounced oscillations in all components, especially in the off-diagonal Jxy and DM terms, indicating that RSOC significantly modulates the spin texture of the indirect exchange.

FIG. 7. Dependence of the normalized RKKY interaction components on the EPC strength λ for several values of the chemical potential (Fermi energy) εF. Parameters are δ = 0.5, α = 0.2 eV·Å, ˜λ/λ = 1/4, ϕR = π/3, and R = 30 Å. Increas- ing εF modifies the oscillation amplitude and period of all RKKY components by altering the Fermi wave vector of the spin-split Rashba bands. Positive (negative) εF shifts, cor- responding to electron (hole) doping, induce phase displace- ments of opposite sign, reflecting the asymmetric contribution of the upper and lower Rashba branches to the exchange.
